summary: I was just about to turn five when Pearl Harbor was bombed in December 1941. I didn’t really understand what was happening at the time, but my older siblings talked about it later. It was a pivotal event that changed our lives, leading to our forced relocation and internment during World War II.

After Pearl Harbor, my family was sent to a camp in Turlock, California. We traveled there by train early one morning, and because our family was so large, we were assigned a stable all to ourselves. It was one of the first places we stayed before moving on to other camps.

We later moved to the Gila River War Relocation Center when I was about five and a half years old. I remember walking my sister to school there and how my parents worked in the mess hall to support us. Life in camp was surprisingly fun for me because there were many kids my age to play with, unlike in Lafayette where I didn’t have many playmates.

My father was born in Japan but raised in Hawaii, so he spoke good English. My mother came from Japan in 1918 and lived on a farm before marrying my father in San Francisco. My mother was left in Japan as a baby and raised by her aunts and grandmother, so she never really lived there again but always felt a connection to her roots.

My father grew up in Hawaii, and later during the war, my family stayed with my grandparents there for two and a half years. My mother’s parents had brought my father and his father to Hawaii, which is how my parents met. We moved back to Berkeley from Hawaii in 1948 after some family changes.
cues: two and a half years stay

summary: My parents met and married in San Francisco after my mother came from Japan in 1918. It was there that they started their family before moving to Lafayette, California. San Francisco was an important place in their early life together.

Lafayette was where I was born and lived before the war. I didn’t have many kids to play with there, unlike in camp. My mother once walked all the way to Upper Happy Valley from Lafayette to visit friends, showing how tough life could be. We took over the home of Mr. and Mrs. Sano, who moved away during that time.
cues: mother’s walk to Upper Happy Valley

summary: My older siblings used to go to the Japanese American hall in Concord almost every Saturday night to watch Japanese movies. It was a place where the community gathered and connected before we were sent to camps. I remember hearing about those times from my older brothers and sisters.

Before the war, my mother grew vegetables to sell at the produce market in Oakland. After we left the camps, some family members moved back to California, including Oakland, where they faced discrimination and were called names at school. Oakland was part of the larger Bay Area community where we lived and worked.

After leaving camp, my father got a job as a potato farmer in Litchfield Park, Arizona, near Glendale. We lived there for about six months in tough conditions, including staying in army tents without running water. My mother didn’t like it there, so she wrote to her mother to help us move to Hawaii.

Glendale was near where we lived in Litchfield Park, Arizona, after the camps. It was close to an Air Force base, but life there was difficult, especially living in tents without proper housing. We only stayed there briefly before moving on to Hawaii.

Near where we lived in Arizona was an Air Force base. My father and brother stayed in the area for several months, but the house they were promised was never finished. The Air Force base was a landmark close to our temporary home after the camps.
cues: Air Force base near Litchfield Park

summary: My father was raised in the Hawaii Territory, which shaped his English skills and upbringing. Later, during the war, we stayed with my grandparents in Hawaii Territory for two and a half years. It was a place of family connection and refuge after the hardships of camp and Arizona.
cues: two and a half years stay

summary: My mother once walked all the way from Lafayette to Upper Happy Valley, about five or six miles, to visit Mr. and Mrs. Sano after they moved there. She took me and my siblings with her, and it was a tough journey. Mrs. Sano later convinced my mother to return home to my father that night.

In 1948, we moved to Berkeley because my oldest sister was left alone with her two boys after her husband passed away. Berkeley became our home after Hawaii, and I went to Berkeley High School where all the Japanese American kids were together. It was a unique experience with little racial tension among us at school.

St. Mary's College High School was one of the two schools in Berkeley at the time, the other being Berkeley High School. It was a private school, and while I attended Berkeley High, it was part of the unique educational landscape for Japanese American students in the area.

I went to Berkeley High School where most of the Japanese American kids attended. We had a special corner at lunchtime called the 'buddha head' corner where we all gathered. I remember there were no major race problems there, and many of us felt accepted among the student body.

I didn’t fully realize the sacrifices of the 442nd Infantry Regiment until I was in my 30s. A friend of mine had several uncles who served in that all Japanese American combat unit during World War II, and luckily they all survived. Their struggle and efforts to gain recognition from the government were something I came to respect deeply.

World War II changed everything for my family and me. After Pearl Harbor, we were forced to leave our home and live in internment camps like Gila River. The war also meant my older siblings and many Japanese Americans served in units like the 442nd Infantry Regiment, facing hardships both abroad and at home.

About seven or eight years ago, I joined the Veterans of Foreign Wars. It was a way for me to connect with others who had served and to honor those, like the 442nd Infantry Regiment, who fought for our country despite the challenges we faced. Joining the VFW helped me appreciate the sacrifices made by Japanese American veterans.
cues: joined seven or eight years ago

summary: The Rohwer War Relocation Center wasn’t a place I stayed at personally, but it was one of the camps where Japanese Americans were sent during the war. I know it was part of the broader system of internment that affected many families like mine. Stories from others who were there helped me understand the full scope of what happened.

Tule Lake was another internment camp used during the war, known for housing those considered disloyal or who resisted the camps. While my family was not sent there, I learned about it as part of the history of Japanese American internment. It represents a difficult chapter in our community’s experience during World War II.

The term 'Kibei' refers to Japanese Americans who were born in the U.S. but educated in Japan. While I didn’t identify as Kibei myself, I was aware of the different experiences within our community. My family’s story was more about being born and raised here and the challenges we faced during the war and after.

Japanese American internment was a defining experience of my childhood. After Pearl Harbor, my family was forced to leave our home and live in camps like Turlock and Gila River. Though I was young and found some joy playing with other kids, my parents and older siblings endured great hardship, and the whole experience left a lasting impact on us.
